basmala: In the Name of God, the Infinitely Merciful, the Most Merciful.
intro: Al-Humaza — “The Slanderer” — condemns in one stroke two evils closely bound: the slander that wounds and the wealth that piles up. But Qushayrī lifts the sūra to another pitch. He sets out a cartography of inner wealth: “Self-sufficiency by what is other than God is poverty; intimacy with what is other than Him is dread; honour by what is other than Him is humiliation.” And he distinguishes five states of soul: “The poor (al-faqīr) is the one who is content with his wealth. The contemptible, the one content with his rank. The bankrupt, the one content with his obedience. The lowly, the one content with what is other than God. And the noble, the one content with God.” The sūra thus says plainly what an outward reading would miss: it is not wealth that is blamed — it is the false self-sufficiency that believes possessions will make one last. Tustarī adds the mystical classification of fires: “There are four fires. The fire of desire, which consumes acts of obedience. The fire of misery, which consumes the profession of Oneness. The fire of severance, which consumes hearts. And the fire of love — that one consumes all other fires.” The Hellfire of the sūra has power only where the love of God is missing; where it burns, says Qushayrī, even Gehenna is put out.

versets.commentaire: Qushayrī gives the nuances: “al-humaza is the one who says what is in a man [an actual fault]; al-lumaza is the one who says what is not in him [pure slander].” Or: humaza wounds by gesture and look, lumaza by speech. In either case, the sūra aims not at an isolated act but at a character become habitual. The harm of slander, for the Sufis, is not merely social — it is a deformation of perception: one no longer sees the other except as matter for judgment, and oneself only as the judging gaze. The opposite of the posture of the servant, who sees himself beneath.

mots.def: preposition li- + kull in the genitive.
humaza: intensive form; one who wounds by gesture or look; or: one who says what is in a man.
lumaza: intensive form; one who wounds with the tongue; or: one who invents what is not.

versets.trad: who amasses wealth and counts it over,
versets.commentaire: jamaʿa (جَمَعَ), he gathered; ʿaddadah (عَدَّدَه), he counted it over. The verb is in the intensive form — he tallies it again and again. Qushayrī, brief but decisive: “He is content with his wealth [instead of being content with God].” And from there the cartography already cited — that which reveals that self-sufficiency through possessions is, in truth, the first form of poverty.

mots.def: ʿaddada: intensive form of ʿadda (to count); to count and recount with care.
mots.gram: perfect verb + suffix pronoun, 3rd pers. masc. sing.

versets.trad: supposing that his wealth makes him immortal.
versets.commentaire: akhlada (أَخْلَدَ), same root as khulūd — eternity, which belongs to God alone and which He grants to whom He will. The greedy man is laughable in two ways: he thinks his wealth will shelter him from death, and he forgets that eternity is elsewhere. Qushayrī sums it: “It keeps him in this lower world.” But this lower world is precisely what passes. The illusion of eternity fixed on what passes is the root of every anguish — the one we hide by checking our accounts.

mots.def: ḥasiba: to think, to believe, to suppose.
akhlada: to render eternal; same root as khulūd, the perpetuity that belongs to God alone.
mots.gram: perfect verb + suffix pronoun, 3rd pers. masc. sing.

versets.trad: Nay! He shall surely be cast into the Crushing Fire.
versets.commentaire: kallā — the Sufi rupture par excellence. Everything before is denied in a single word. al-Ḥuṭama (الحُطَمَة) comes from ḥaṭama — to break, to crush, to shatter. It is a Name of the Fire, but also an image: the soul that has made itself brittle by clinging to goods will be crushed by what it clung to. The Fire, in Sufi reading, is not only a place — it is the very condition of a soul that confused God with its goods.

mots.def: refutation particle: nay, by no means.
nabadha: to throw, to cast; energetic passive.
mots.gram: energetic passive imperfect, 3rd pers. masc. sing.

mots.def: al-Ḥuṭama: a Name of the Fire; intensive form of ḥaṭama (to crush, to shatter).

versets.trad: And what shall apprise you of what the Crushing Fire is?
versets.commentaire: A typically Quranic question. What follows is not a definition: the nature of al-Ḥuṭama exceeds the human mind. The sūra will drop, in a few words, an image that says more than any speech.

mots.gram: perfect verb + suffix pronoun, 3rd pers. masc. sing.

versets.trad: It is the Fire of God, never extinguished,
versets.commentaire: al-mūqada (المُوقَدَة), the ceaselessly stoked fire. Tustarī here drops his typology: “There are four fires. The fire of desire (shahwa), which consumes acts of obedience. The fire of misery (shaqāwa), which consumes the profession of Oneness. The fire of severance (qaṭīʿa), which consumes hearts. And the fire of love (maḥabba) — that one consumes all other fires.” To understand al-Ḥuṭama, one must know which of these fires are already burning in a soul. And one must know which — only one — extinguishes the others.

mots.def: mūqada: passive participle of awqada; ceaselessly stoked.

versets.commentaire: al-afʾida (الأَفْئِدَة) — plural of fuʾād, the heart in its finest point, the centre of feeling. Tustarī: “The Fire does not die down by consuming the skin and the flesh, but its heat reaches the very hearts.” Here the sūra strikes most truly: the fire that crushes is not a fire from outside — it rises up to the very place from which greed, slander, illusion came forth. And that is why one must fear, not the outside, but what one has nurtured within.

mots.def: fem. sing. relative pronoun (referring to al-Ḥuṭama).
iṭṭalaʿa: to climb up to, to reach the top of.
fuʾād: the heart in its subtle centre; plural afʾida.

versets.commentaire: muʾṣada (مُؤْصَدَة): closed, sealed. The Fire is not an open plain — it is a closed enclosure. And the Sufi reading specifies: what closes the door is not God — it is the soul itself which, by sealing itself upon what is not Him, no longer has the possibility of opening. The door closes from within, by habit.

mots.def: emphasis particle inna + feminine suffix pronoun.
muʾṣada: passive participle of awṣada; sealed, shut, closed.

versets.commentaire: ʿamad mumaddada (عَمَد مُمَدَّدَة): pillars stretched out, like the enclosure of a fire holding itself in. But Qushayrī closes the sūra on a word of unexpected beauty: “It is shown that when knowledge (maʿrifa, مَعْرِفَة) takes fire in the heart of the believer, it burns within him every request and every claim. That is why Gehenna, on that Day, will say to the believer: pass, O believer — for your light has put out my flame!” A light, in man, can extinguish the fire of Hell. The sūra, which seemed only a warning, ends therefore on the announcement of this light — which is the love of God alive in the heart.

mots.def: ʿamad: plural of ʿamūd, pillar, column; here the pillars of the Fire.
mumaddada: intensive passive participle of madda; stretched, drawn out.
